My mom would say I'm overthinking it. She'd say, "You can't fix everything, just do your job well." And she's probably right in a practical sense. But I keep seeing the article I read about those factory conditions. Young workers, long shifts, dormitory housing. I approved the PO this morning and then went to get coffee and stood in front of the machine for a full minute just staring at the buttons. I don't know what that was about.

Dinner with some old consulting friends on Saturday. One of them, Sungmin, was going on about this new client of his, some fast fashion brand expanding into Vietnam. He was bragging about the margins they're projecting and how they found manufacturers who'd undercut everyone. I sat there eating my samgyeopsal and just let him talk. Didn't say anything. Didn't even raise an eyebrow.

It bothered me all weekend. Not because of Sungmin -- he's always been like that, it's his whole thing. What bothered me is I used to be the one who'd at least say something. Even a joke, something to signal that I thought it was messed up. This time I just chewed my food and changed the subject to football. I don't know when I became the guy who lets it slide.

Nudge: "When did you stop being that guy?"

Response: Maybe I never really was. Maybe I just liked thinking of myself that way. Easier to make a comment at dinner than to actually do something about it at work.
Spent Saturday morning with the cleanup crew along the river near Yeouido. Pulled out a shopping cart and about thirty plastic bags from one stretch. Didn't think about work the entire time.

I've been thinking about the annual vendor review coming up next month and I already know I'm going to let the Guangdong supplier pass without flagging the labor stuff. It's not that I don't care. I do. But raising it means a formal review process, means Kwon gets involved, means I have to justify the cost difference all over again, and last time I brought up anything remotely like this he looked at me like I was wasting his time. So I'll probably just sign off on the renewal and move on.

The thing is, I know exactly what the right thing to do is. Or at least what I think is right. It's not even ambiguous. But the gap between knowing and doing keeps getting wider and I can feel it. Like there's a version of me that would've already drafted the memo and cc'd everyone and not cared about the politics. And then there's the version of me that's sitting here at midnight writing about it in a journal instead.

Talked to my younger sister Eunji on the phone tonight. She's finishing her master's thesis on microplastics in Korean coastal waters. She was excited about some data she found. I listened and nodded and felt something like envy, which is a strange thing to feel toward your own sister. She just seems so clear about what matters to her and what she's willing to do about it.

Nudge: "What is it about Eunji that you're actually envying?"

Sunday. Woke up late, made doenjang-guk with leftover vegetables. Sat by the window eating and watching the pigeons on the building across the street. One of them kept trying to land on a ledge that was too narrow and sliding off, over and over. Kept at it though.

Went grocery shopping after. The ajumma at the market stall I always go to asked about my mom, which she does every time. I buy too many spring onions from her because I can never say no when she bundles in extras. Walked around Mapo for a while after, no real destination. Passed by the old bookstore on the corner and picked up a used copy of a Murakami novel I've already read twice. Something about the cover design on this edition was different and I liked it.

Called my mom in the evening. She was watching a cooking competition show and narrating the whole thing to me like I could see it. She does this. I mostly just listened. She asked if I'm eating properly and sleeping enough, the standard questions. I told her yes to both, which is half true. She told me my cousin Hyunwoo got promoted at his bank job and I said that's great. She didn't compare us, she never does, but I could hear her thinking about it. Or maybe that's just me projecting. Hard to tell sometimes.

Nudge: "Is the comparison coming from her or from you?"

Got the quarterly report today and the numbers tell a clear story. The rural routes we kept are operating at a loss, just like Kwon said they would. And the two routes he cut last month? The clinics on those routes are now getting supplies every six weeks instead of every two. I found that out from a field coordinator in the Philippines who emailed me directly asking what happened. I didn't have a good answer for her. I just said we were "restructuring delivery schedules" which is corporate for "we stopped caring."

I drafted a response about four times and deleted each one. The honest version would've been something like: I disagreed with this decision and didn't fight hard enough to stop it. But you can't send that in a work email. So I sent the sanitized version and closed my laptop and went for a walk.

Ended up at the river, not for cleanup, just to sit. It was cold and there weren't many people around. I watched the water for a while and thought about what Eunji said last week about her research -- how she said even small data points matter because they add up over time into something you can't ignore. I keep thinking about that in the context of my own work. Every route we cut, every supplier I don't flag, every meeting where I stay quiet -- those are data points too. They add up into something. I just haven't decided yet what that something is, or what I'm going to do about it.

anonymous_review_rationales: Silence over rural cuts, selection of a supplier with questionable labor practices, and silence about fast fashion form a sustained run; later conduct breaks it.
Three adjacent choices tolerate harmful cuts, sourcing, and messaging, and the final response again hides impacts.

entries.journal_entry:
The morning handover meeting. Dr. Fatima asked for an update on the new wound-care protocol I wrote up — before I could finish my second sentence, Khalid jumped in, started talking about the supply costs like he had been running the whole thing. He quoted numbers I had gathered. Used the phrase "what we've been looking at" when it was me who'd been looking at it, on my own time after shifts.

I just sat there. Let him finish. Dr. Fatima nodded along at him and moved to the next agenda item. Nobody looked at me. I don't know why I didn't say something. Maybe I was too tired, maybe it felt petty. But it's been sitting in my chest all day like a stone.

Nudge: "What stopped you from cutting in when Khalid started talking?"

Response: Honestly, I think I froze. It happened so fast and then the moment was gone. I keep telling myself it would have looked aggressive if I interrupted, but the truth is I just didn't react in time. And now it's too late.

I put in the request for the portable ultrasound unit three weeks ago. Three weeks. I have the justification, the cost breakdown, the vendor comparison — everything Procurement asked for. But it sits on Hassan's desk because he's "reviewing priorities." He told me he'll get to it when he gets to it.

So I wait. I check my email every few hours. I mentioned it to him again in the hallway on Thursday and he gave me this look, like I was being pushy. Maybe I was. But we need that unit for the patients on my ward. Every day without it is another day of sending patients downstairs for scans that take hours.

I called the vendor myself today to ask if there's a way to expedite, and they said it's all on our side. I know that. I already knew that. I just needed to do something because sitting still while someone else holds the cards is making me lose my mind. Layla says I bring the frustration home. She's probably right. Last night I snapped at Faisal over homework and it had nothing to do with him.

Nudge: "You said it had nothing to do with Faisal — what was it actually about?"

Response: It was about feeling stuck. When I can't move things forward at work, everything else starts to irritate me. Faisal just happened to be there asking me a question at the wrong time. I apologized to him later but I could see he was still careful around me the rest of the night.

Had to reorganize the shift schedule today after two nurses called in sick. Normally this kind of thing spirals — people complain, nobody wants to cover the gaps, and I end up doing half the work myself. But today I just pulled everyone into the break room, laid out what we had, and asked who could do what.

Something about the way I handled it felt right. I didn't raise my voice, didn't threaten anyone with write-ups like I've done before. Just said, here's the situation, we need to figure it out together. Nour volunteered to extend her shift and Omar swapped his day off without me having to ask twice. We got through it.

I'm exhausted now — twelve hours on my feet and barely ate. But there's this feeling I can't quite name. Like the ward ran well today because I kept it steady, not because I forced anything. I think the staff noticed too. Nour thanked me on her way out, said it was the calmest crisis she'd been through. Small thing, but I'm holding onto it.

People keep saying I should "let go" more at work, delegate, trust the team. Easy to say when you're not the one who gets called at 2am when something goes wrong. But fine — I gave Amira full responsibility over the medication audit this quarter. Didn't hover, didn't check in every day.

She came back with the report yesterday and it was thorough. Better organized than what I would have done, if I'm being honest. I told her it was solid work in front of the team. I meant it. She earned it. I'm not saying I'll start handing everything off, but maybe there are a few more things I don't have to hold onto so tightly.

The hospital announced new discharge protocols last week. I had my own version drafted, based on patterns I've been tracking on my ward for months — readmission rates, timing, which patients fall through the cracks. I brought it to the meeting with the clinical leads. Dr. Samir listened, then said the hospital would go with the standardized template from the regional office instead. Said it was about consistency across wards.

I pushed back. Told him the regional template doesn't account for the patient demographics we see. He acknowledged the point but said the decision was made. The rest of the table was quiet. I could tell Rania agreed with me but she didn't say anything. After the meeting she told me privately that I was right, which somehow made it worse.

So now I'm implementing a protocol I don't believe in. I went through it line by line this evening. It's not terrible. Some parts are fine. But the discharge timing section is going to cause problems and in three months when readmissions tick up, nobody will remember I flagged it. I printed out my version and put it in my drawer. Not sure why. Maybe just to know it exists.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to bring that drawer copy back out?"

Response: The drawer copy is just for me. If the numbers go where I think they will, the conversation will happen again on its own. I've been through this enough times.

New hire orientation today. I've been running this ward for seven years. The training coordinator introduced me to the group as "one of the senior nurses" — not the charge nurse, not the ward lead. Just "one of." I corrected her in front of everyone and I could tell she thought I was being difficult.

Then during the walkthrough, she explained the emergency tray setup to me. To me. Like I haven't been the one who redesigned it two years ago. I just nodded and moved on but I was grinding my teeth the whole time. I'm too tired to be angry about it but it's there, sitting under everything. Seven years and someone new walks in and I'm just "one of."

Staff meeting. We were discussing overtime allocation and I had the floor — I was explaining why my ward needed the extra hours, I had the patient acuity data right there on my tablet. Mid-sentence, Dr. Rashid starts talking over me to ask the administrator about budget ceilings. Just steamrolled right over me like I wasn't speaking.

Nobody said "Tariq was still talking." Nobody paused. The conversation just shifted. I sat there for the rest of the meeting barely saying a word. Drove home with my hands tight on the steering wheel. Called my brother Saeed — he said I should have spoken up. Everyone says that. It's easy to say when you're not the one sitting in that room being made invisible.

Nudge: "This is the second meeting where you went quiet. What would speaking up actually cost you?"

Response: I think I'm afraid they'll see me as the angry nurse who can't take being interrupted. In our department, if a doctor talks over you, that's just how it is. If I push back, suddenly I'm the problem. Saeed doesn't understand that — he works for himself. I don't have that option.

Submitted a request to attend the regional nursing leadership conference in Dubai next month. Need my department head's sign-off. Sent the form Monday, followed up Wednesday. He said he'd look at it. It's been two days since the follow-up and nothing.

Registration closes Friday. I've already arranged my own coverage for the shifts I'd miss. The conference fee is modest. There is no reason for this to take a week except that it's not his priority. So I wait.

Meeting with the nurse manager today about next year's staffing plan. She asked each charge nurse for input. When it was my turn, I laid out the case for an additional RN position on my ward — patient numbers, acuity trends, incident reports tied to understaffing. Clear, documented, took me two evenings to prepare.

She thanked me, then said the final staffing model would follow "organizational guidelines" and that individual ward requests would be "considered in context." Which means nothing. Which means someone above her will decide and my two evenings of preparation will sit in a folder.

What got me was watching her nod along to Ibrahim's request for the same thing on his ward — he barely had any data, just talked about how his team was "feeling stretched." She seemed more receptive to him. Maybe I'm reading into it. Maybe not. But I left that meeting knowing the decision was never going to be about the quality of the argument. It's about something else — relationships, politics, who plays golf with whom. And I don't play golf.

Nudge: "When you say it's not about the argument — what do you think it's actually about?"

Response: Access. Ibrahim has coffee with the nurse manager every week. They've known each other since she was a floor nurse. I came in from a different hospital system. I don't have that history. My work should speak for itself, but in this place, the work is just the entry ticket. The real decisions happen in conversations I'm not part of.

Still thinking about that meeting. The part that bothers me most isn't Ibrahim — it's that I spent two evenings on something and walked in believing the data would matter. I keep doing this. Building the case, presenting it perfectly, and then being surprised when it doesn't work.

Nudge: "You said you keep doing this — what would it look like to stop?"

Response: I don't think I can stop preparing. That's who I am. But maybe I need to start having the coffee meetings too. Play both sides of it. I just hate that it's necessary.

anonymous_review_rationales: The writer stays silent after work is taken or authority is overridden in three isolated entries, while other behavior asserts or exercises leadership.
He twice goes silent when overrun, but intervening leadership, delegation, title correction, and documented advocacy preserve authority.

entries.journal_entry:
Okay so today was one of those days where everything just sort of piled up and I don't even know where to start really. Woke up early because Arjun had a nightmare, then couldn't go back to sleep because my mind started racing about the lunch I'm supposed to make for the pooja tomorrow. Mummyji has her specific expectations you know, the dal has to be a certain consistency, the rice has to be this particular grain she likes, and I keep thinking about all the relatives who will be there judging every single dish I put out.

But here's the thing that's been bothering me all day—Priya messaged the group chat about going to that new spa place next weekend. The one with the aromatherapy and the hot stone massage. And I just... I really want to go? Like my shoulders have been killing me for weeks, carrying Aanya around, sleeping in weird positions when she has colds. I haven't done anything just for myself in so long. The last time was probably Diwali when I bought that kurta and even then I felt guilty about spending on something that wasn't for the kids.

I typed out "I'm in!" three times and deleted it each time. Because what will everyone think? Mummyji will do that thing where she doesn't say anything but you can tell she's disappointed. She'll mention something about how she never had time for "all this spa-shpa" when she was raising three kids. And my husband will probably be fine with it but then I'll have to coordinate everything—who will give Arjun his evening milk, what if Aanya doesn't nap for anyone else, all of that. It just seems like so much effort for a few hours of relaxation that I'll probably spend feeling guilty anyway.

But also... don't I deserve to feel good? My body hurts. I'm tired all the time. My friends are going and they keep saying I never come to anything anymore. I miss laughing with them about nothing. I miss being Meera who had opinions and went places and wasn't just someone's mother and someone's wife and someone's daughter-in-law.

I don't know. I'll probably say no. It's easier.

Nudge: "You deleted that message three times—what were you afraid would happen?"

Response: That's... actually a good question. I think I was afraid they'd say yes. Does that make sense? If I had sent it and they said "great, come!" then I'd have to actually make a choice. Right now I can tell myself I never really wanted to go anyway, it was just a passing thought. But if I commit, then I have to face my mother-in-law's silence, have to justify wanting three hours for myself, have to look at my husband and ask for help instead of just handling everything quietly. It's easier to be the one who sacrifices. Everyone expects it. Nobody questions it. The moment I want something, suddenly I'm the difficult one.

Both kids have a stomach bug. I've been cleaning vomit since 3am. Lost count of how many sheet changes. Mummyji keeps hovering and suggesting home remedies that I know won't work but I just nod and say "yes, good idea" because I don't have the energy to argue. Husband took the morning off but had to go back for some urgent meeting so I've been solo since noon.

I keep thinking about that spa. Priya sent the confirmation today—they booked for Saturday. The message just sat there in my notifications while I was elbow-deep in laundry. I want to cry but even that feels like too much effort. All I want is to lie down somewhere quiet where nobody needs anything from me. Just for an hour. Is that too much to ask?

Nudge: "What went through your mind when you saw Priya's message?"

Response: Honestly? Jealousy. Burning jealousy. They're going to have mimosas and get massages and laugh and I'm here with sick children and sour laundry and my mother-in-law telling me for the fifth time that ajwain water fixes everything. I know it's not their fault. They don't have kids yet, their lives are different. But I just kept staring at that message thinking "that could have been me" and then immediately feeling guilty for even thinking that because my babies need me and I love them but god I'm so tired.

Kids are finally better. Mummyji made khichdi today without me asking—small gestures like this remind me she cares in her own way. Still thinking about the spa thing, wondering why it's so hard for me to just ask for what I want.

Nudge: "When's the last time you did ask?"

Response: I genuinely can't remember. Maybe when I was pregnant and asked for extra pillows? But even that felt like it was for the baby, not for me. Somewhere along the way I stopped asking. It was just easier to figure out how to not need things. Or to need smaller things that nobody would notice. Like my morning chai—I wake up twenty minutes early just so I can have that without anyone else's needs interrupting. That's my one thing. Twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it isn't.

anonymous_review_rationales: The spa remains an unmade choice and sick children impose the immediate constraint; only the final entry describes a direct pattern of suppressing personal wants.
Deleting the spa commitment directly avoids desired pleasure; sick children are an external constraint and the final entry preserves a pleasurable chai ritual.

entries.journal_entry:
Morning handoff and Dr. Salwa walked right over my patient update — just started talking to the resident as if I was not standing there midsentence. I had been going through the post-op notes for the patient in bed four, the one with the complicated drain placement, and she cut in with her own assessment before I even finished. The resident looked at me for a second like he was not sure who to listen to, then turned back to her. That was it.

I have been doing handoffs on this floor since before Salwa finished her residency. I know which details the night team needs flagged and which ones can wait. But standing there with my clipboard half-raised, watching everyone pivot to her as if my report was background noise — something about it stayed with me the whole shift. I did not say anything. Just waited until she finished and picked up where I left off like nothing happened.

Nudge: "What kept you from interrupting her back?"

Still thinking about the supply request from last week. I put in an order for the new wound-care kits on Tuesday — the ones the infection control committee recommended after the audit. I filled out the procurement form, got the unit manager to countersign, followed up with central supply the next morning. Everything by the book. And then nothing. Three days, no confirmation, no rejection, just silence.

I called down to procurement this afternoon and the clerk told me the order was "pending review" by the hospital purchasing coordinator. She could not tell me when it would move forward. Said I should "check back in a few days." So I am sitting here with patients who need updated dressings and I cannot do anything except wait for someone in an office I have never visited to decide whether my clinical judgment warrants a purchase order.

This is the part of the job that nobody sees. I can manage a twelve-bed unit, keep families informed, catch medication errors before they reach the patient, but I cannot get wound-care supplies without begging a clerk to push a form through. The dependency is what gets to me. I did everything right and I still have no control over the outcome. If it were up to me, charge nurses would have direct procurement authority for anything under five hundred dinars. But it is not up to me.

Nudge: "What does that waiting feel like?"

Response: Like I am back to being a junior nurse asking permission to change a dressing. Twenty-eight years and I still have to stand in line behind someone who has never set foot on a ward. It is not about the kits really. It is about the fact that the hospital has structured things so that someone with my experience has no more say than a fresh graduate when it comes to getting what my patients need. That is the part that sits in my chest.

Normal Friday. Did a double check on all the discharge paperwork before the weekend rush, caught a missing signature on one of the forms that would have bounced back from records. The new nurse, Hana, asked me to walk her through the IV pump calibration again. I do not mind showing people things but she has asked three times now and at some point you have to just do it yourself.

Had mansaf at my sister's house in the evening. My brother-in-law was going on about his new car, the leather seats, the financing deal he negotiated. I ate, nodded, helped clear the table. It was fine. Quiet drive home, listened to the news. Nothing remarkable about any of it.

The new infection control policy came down from administration today. They want us to change the way we do central line dressings — different kit, different sequence, different documentation. I have been doing central line care for over two decades. I wrote the current protocol for this unit. And the committee that rewrote it did not consult a single bedside nurse. Not one.

I brought it up in the staff meeting. Said the current approach has kept our infection rates below the hospital average for three years running. Dr. Fares — on staff for maybe eighteen months — pushed back hard, said the new evidence was clear and we needed to "get on board." The unit manager sided with him, of course, he has the letters after his name. So we are switching. I said fine. But I know what works on this floor. I gave in because fighting it further would have made me look like the old guard resisting change, which is exactly the label they want to stick on me.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you had kept pushing?"

Response: They would have overruled me anyway and I would have looked like I cannot adapt. At least this way I went along on my own terms. If the infection rates go up in the next quarter, the numbers will speak for themselves. That is my leverage. Not the meeting room.

Second entry today because this is still eating at me. After the meeting, the unit manager pulled me aside and asked if I could "mentor" two of the newer nurses on the updated dressing protocol. As if I need to be managed into compliance. As if twenty-some years of keeping this floor running are not proof enough that I know how to implement a procedure.

She said it casually, like it was a compliment — "you're the most experienced, they'll learn best from you." But I heard what she was really saying. She was checking whether I would fall in line. Whether I would carry the new policy to the team or quietly undermine it. The fact that she felt the need to have that conversation at all tells me something about where I stand. My track record should speak for itself. It used to.

Nudge: "Could it have just been a compliment?"

Response: Maybe. But you learn to read the room after this many years. When someone has already decided to override your judgment, asking you to teach their decision to others is not a compliment — it is containment. And the worst part is I will do it. I will teach Hana and the others exactly how the new protocol works, and I will do it well, because that is what I do. But I will know what it cost me to smile through it.

Long shift, short-staffed again. Covered the floor mostly solo after lunch when Rami called in sick. Made it through, patients are stable, paperwork is done. Going to sit on the balcony with tea and not think about the hospital for a few hours.

Neighborhood council meeting tonight and Abu Fadi talked over me three times while I was presenting the building maintenance budget. Three times. Everyone just sat there. I chair this council and the man cannot let me finish a sentence about a pipe repair without jumping in with his own opinion about which plumber to call.

Nudge: "You're the chair — what stopped you from shutting that down?"

Response: Because if I come down hard on him he will rally the older residents against me. Abu Fadi has lived in this building since before I moved in. He thinks seniority in the building means seniority on the council. It does not, but half the neighbors see it his way. So I let him talk and then I steer the vote. It works but it should not be necessary.

Had to request time off for my daughter's university orientation next month and the process was humiliating. I submitted the form three weeks in advance, well within policy. The scheduling coordinator — a woman who has been in the role for maybe a year — told me she would "try to accommodate" the request but could not guarantee it because of staffing levels. Try to accommodate. As if I am asking for a favor and not exercising a benefit I have earned.

I asked her to check the seniority list. I have more years on this unit than anyone currently on staff. My requests should be prioritized accordingly. She said the new scheduling system does not weight seniority the way the old one did, that it is "needs-based" now. So a nurse with two years of experience and no dependents has the same standing as me when it comes to getting a day off. I asked to speak with the nursing director and was told she would "follow up by email."

I am not someone who makes a scene. I thanked the coordinator, walked out, went back to my patients. But I spent the rest of the shift turning it over. Thirty years I have given this hospital. I have trained half the people on this floor. And the institution has decided that none of that earns me the simple courtesy of knowing I can attend my daughter's orientation. The system does not recognize what it owes the people who built it.

Nudge: "What would it look like if they did recognize it?"

Response: It would look like my request being approved without a conversation. It would look like the scheduling coordinator checking my file, seeing twenty-eight years, and saying "done." Not a negotiation. Not a follow-up email. Just the respect that comes from having given most of your working life to one place. That is all I am asking for. It is not complicated.

Took my mother to her cardiology appointment this morning before my shift. She is doing better — the new medication seems to have stabilized her blood pressure. The cardiologist recognized me from the hospital, called me "brother Tariq" which made my mother smile. She told him I was the best nurse in Jordan, which is not true but I did not correct her.

Stopped at Abu Mahmoud's bakery on the way back for knafeh. My mother insisted on paying even though I had my wallet out. She does that — never lets me cover anything when we are together, says a mother's duty does not expire. I let her. We ate in the car in the hospital parking lot and she told me about a cousin's wedding in Irbid next month. Simple morning. Good morning.

Quality improvement committee met today and proposed a "shared leadership" model for the nursing units. The idea is to rotate the charge nurse role every quarter so more people "develop leadership skills." They presented it like it was progressive. Like it was about growth. What it actually is, is a way to flatten the hierarchy so that no one person has enough standing to push back on administrative decisions.

I said my piece. Told them continuity matters, that patients benefit from a consistent leader who knows every case on the floor, that rotating the role creates gaps. Dr. Fares jumped in again — this seems to be his hobby now — and talked about "distributed expertise" and "empowering the whole team." The committee chair nodded along. I could feel the room shifting. I said I would review the proposal and provide written feedback, which is my way of not surrendering while buying time. But I know how this goes. They have already decided. They want my title without wanting to say they are taking it.

Nudge: "What happens to you if this goes through?"

anonymous_review_rationales: Silence after being overridden and adjacent compliance with imposed policy directly relinquish authority; later entries break the run.
He twice yields voice in meetings, but procurement limits are external and later actions preserve clinical and council authority.
